original_abstract: The properties of nitrogen centres acting either as hydrogen-bond or Brønsted acceptors in solid molecular acid-base complexes have been probed by N 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as well as (15)N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy and are interpreted with reference to local crystallographic structure information provided by X-ray diffraction (XRD). We have previously shown that the strong chemical shift of the N 1s binding energy associated with the protonation of nitrogen centres unequivocally distinguishes protonated (salt) from hydrogen-bonded (co-crystal) nitrogen species. This result is further supported by significant ssNMR shifts to low frequency, which occur with proton transfer from the acid to the base component. Generally, only minor chemical shifts occur upon co-crystal formation, unless a strong hydrogen bond is formed. CASTEP density functional theory (DFT) calculations of (15)N ssNMR isotropic chemical shifts correlate well with the experimental data, confirming that computational predictions of H-bond strengths and associated ssNMR chemical shifts allow the identification of salt and co-crystal structures (NMR crystallography). The excellent agreement between the conclusions drawn by XPS and the combined CASTEP/ssNMR investigations opens up a reliable avenue for local structure characterization in molecular systems even in the absence of crystal structure information, for example for non-crystalline or amorphous matter. The range of 17 different systems investigated in this study demonstrates the generic nature of this approach, which will be applicable to many other molecular materials in organic, physical, and materials chemistry.
humanized_abstract: To our knowledge, the properties of nitrogen centres acting as hydrogen-bond or Brønsted acceptor in solid molecular acid-base complexes have not been investigated. Using XPS (N 1s), solid-state (15)N NMR spectroscopy, we correlated the effects of acid-base pairing within various molecular solids, and related the observed spectral changes to their local crystallographic structure, as revealed by XRD. We have previously shown that the large difference in N 1s binding energy arising with the protonation of nitrogen centres unambiguously enables salts versus hydrogen-bonded (co-crystal) nitrogen species to be distinguished. In conjunction with large (15)N NMR shift decreases following the protonation of the nitrogen-centre base (arising upon transfer of the acid H-atom to the nitrogen-centre base), this differentiates salts from their hydrogen-bonded equivalents more than a few hundred ppm. In contrast, generally relatively small changes occur in the (15)N NMR spectra upon acid-base complexation, except where these are especially strong. Our CASTEP-DFT simulations of the (15)N ssNMR spectra correlate accurately with experiment and these find, for each of the acid-base complexes, that their computed ssNMR H-bond strength to nitrogen-centre sites and the associated (15)N shifts afford clear information as to salts or hydrogen-bonded co-crystals (NMR crystallography). A good agreement is also achieved when relating the XPS and CASTEP-DFT data for acid-base complexed systems, enabling potential identification of the local structure within molecular systems lacking XRD crystalline information; for example in the amorphous state or for non-crystalline solids. Finally, a large set of 17 differing systems is used to demonstrate the general applicability of our method, opening access to determining their properties through experimentation in organic, physical and materials chemistry.
